longDescription:
Shandong Province, China Tai Shan (Mount Tai) is the most sacred of China's Five Great Mountains and the foremost symbol of
                Chinese civilization. Rising 1,545 meters (5,069 feet) in Shandong Province, it has been a place
                of imperial worship for over 3,000 years. From the Qin Dynasty to the Qing, 72 emperors made
                pilgrimage to its summit to perform the Feng and Shan sacrifices - the most important rituals
                of the Chinese state, reporting to Heaven and Earth on their reign and receiving divine mandate.
                Confucius climbed it, Qin Shi Huang inscribed its rocks, and countless poets, philosophers, and
                pilgrims have sought enlightenment on its ancient stone stairways. The Chinese saying goes:
                "He who climbs Tai Shan will live to be a hundred."

name: Tai Shan (Mount Tai)
description:
Explore temples, mountains, groves, and holy sites that have served as gateways between the human and divine realms
                across cultures and millennia. From ancient stone circles to living pilgrimage destinations, discover the places
                where heaven touches earth.
significance:
Shandong Province, China Tai Shan (Mount Tai) is the most sacred of China's Five Great Mountains and the foremost symbol of
                Chinese civilization. Rising 1,545 meters (5,069 feet) in Shandong Province, it has been a place
                of imperial worship for over 3,000 years.

geography: Elevated terrain with commanding views; often snow-capped or shrouded in mist, symbolizing proximity to the divine

keyMyths.title: The Eastern Peak and the Five Sacred Mountains
keyMyths.description: Tai Shan is revered as the foremost of the Five Sacred Mountains of China, each associated with a cardinal direction. It is believed to be the place where emperors communicated with Heaven through elaborate Feng Shan sacrifices to legitimize their rule and ensure prosperity for the empire.
keyMyths.source: Records of the Grand Historian (Shiji), Sima Qian

keyMyths.title: The Daughter of the Jade Emperor and the Stone Girl
keyMyths.description: Popular folklore tells of a daughter of the Jade Emperor (the supreme deity in Taoism) who descended to Tai Shan and became a benevolent goddess known as the Bixia Yuanjun or the Azure Cloud Princess. She is particularly venerated by women seeking fertility and safe childbirth, and her temple atop Tai Shan is a major pilgrimage site.
keyMyths.source: Dongyue Taishan Zhi (Gazetteer of Mount Tai)

keyMyths.title: Journey to the Underworld
keyMyths.description: Ancient beliefs associated Tai Shan with the realm of the dead. It was thought that souls of the deceased would gather at the foot of the mountain before their journey to the underworld, ruled over by a deity associated with Tai Shan. This connection to the afterlife contributed to the mountain's sacred status.

keyMyths.title: Confucius' Ascent of Tai Shan
keyMyths.description: Confucius is said to have climbed Tai Shan, an event that solidified the mountain's importance in Chinese culture and philosophy. Accounts suggest that from the summit, Confucius gained a broader perspective on the world and humanity, reinforcing the mountain's reputation as a place of enlightenment.

keyMyths.title: Emperor Qin Shi Huang's Sacrifice
keyMyths.description: Qin Shi Huang, the first emperor of China, performed the Feng and Shan sacrifices on Tai Shan, attempting to claim divine mandate for his dynasty. His elaborate ritual, though ultimately unsuccessful in securing his dynasty's longevity, highlights the enduring significance of the mountain as a site of imperial power and cosmic connection.
keyMyths.source: Records of the Grand Historian (Shiji), Sima Qian

sources.name: Records of the Grand Historian (Shiji), Sima Qian
sources.description: A comprehensive historical text covering the history of China up to the Han dynasty, including accounts of imperial rituals on Mount Tai.
sources.title: Records of the Grand Historian (Shiji), Sima Qian

sources.name: Dongyue Taishan Zhi (Gazetteer of Mount Tai)
sources.description: A detailed gazetteer dedicated solely to Mount Tai, providing information on its history, geography, temples, and legends.
sources.title: Dongyue Taishan Zhi (Gazetteer of Mount Tai)

sources.name: Huainanzi
sources.description: An ancient Chinese philosophical text that discusses cosmology, mythology, and the relationship between humans and the natural world, including references to Mount Tai's connection to the afterlife.
sources.title: Huainanzi

sources.name: The Analects of Confucius
sources.description: A collection of sayings and ideas attributed to the Chinese philosopher Confucius, containing accounts of his life and travels, including his ascent of Mount Tai.
sources.title: The Analects of Confucius

sources.name: Taishan: China's Sacred Mountain
sources.description: A modern academic study focusing on the cultural and religious significance of Mount Tai throughout Chinese history.
sources.title: Taishan: China's Sacred Mountain
